phrases: Dank je wel — Thank you very much
Spreekt u Engels? — Do you speak English?
Always ring your bike bell when approaching pedestrians or other cyclists
Don't walk in the red bike lanes - they're for bicycles only and cyclists have right of way
Tipping 10% is appreciated but not mandatory; rounding up the bill is common practice
safety_tips: EMERGENCY CONTACTS: Police, Fire, Ambulance: 112 (free from any phone)
Indian Embassy in Netherlands: Address - Buitenrustweg 2, 2517 KD The Hague, Phone: +31-70-346-9771, Email: hoc.hague@mea.gov.in
Indian Embassy Emergency Contact: +31-6-5340-9801 (24/7 emergency helpline)
Non-emergency Police: 0900-8844, Tourist Police: Available at Central Station and major tourist areas
Medical Emergency: If you need medical help, go to nearest hospital or call 112. Major hospitals include Amsterdam UMC and OLVG
COMMON SCAMS: Fake police asking for passport/wallet checks - real police show ID and don't handle money
Bicycle rental scams: Only rent from official shops, avoid street vendors offering cheap deals
Pickpocketing hotspots: Trams, Central Station, Dam Square, Red Light District - keep valuables secure
ATM skimming: Use ATMs inside banks, cover PIN entry, check for unusual attachments on card slot
Overcharging at restaurants/bars: Check prices before ordering, especially in tourist areas
Fake drug dealers: Avoid anyone offering drugs on street - it's illegal and often fake/dangerous substances
PRACTICAL SAFETY: Always carry ID (passport or copy), keep originals in hotel safe
Transportation safety: Validate tickets on trams/buses, be aware of pickpockets during rush hours
Cycling safety: Follow bike lanes, use lights at night, lock bikes properly with two locks
Water safety: Don't swim in canals - they're polluted and have strong currents
Weather preparedness: Carry umbrella/rain jacket, weather changes quickly
Money safety: Use contactless payments when possible, don't carry large amounts of cash
Accommodation: Book through verified platforms, check reviews, confirm booking before arrival
Red Light District: Stay on main streets, don't take photos of workers, be extra cautious at night
Drug policy awareness: Cannabis is tolerated in coffee shops but illegal elsewhere, hard drugs are strictly prohibited
Tourist Information: VVV Amsterdam tourist offices can help with directions and legitimate services
Language: Most Dutch speak English, but learn basic Dutch phrases for emergencies
Insurance: Ensure travel insurance covers medical emergencies and theft
Cultural sensitivity: Respect local customs, don't block bike lanes while walking
Emergency apps: Download 112NL app for location-based emergency services

connectivity_tips: Purchase a prepaid SIM card from major Dutch providers like KPN, Vodafone NL, or T-Mobile Netherlands at Amsterdam Schiphol Airport upon arrival - available at official stores in arrivals hall
KPN offers excellent coverage across Netherlands with tourist SIM cards starting from €15-20 for 1GB data valid for 30 days, available at KPN stores and authorized retailers
Vodafone Netherlands provides prepaid SIM cards from €10-25 with data packages ranging from 1GB to unlimited, purchasable at Vodafone stores, supermarkets like Albert Heijn, and electronics stores
T-Mobile NL offers competitive prepaid plans starting €15 for 2GB monthly data with good 4G/5G coverage in Amsterdam and major cities
Consider eSIM options like Airalo, Holafly, or Nomad which offer Netherlands/Europe plans from €4-15 for 1-10GB, easily activated before or during travel without visiting physical stores
Buy SIM cards at convenience stores, supermarkets (Albert Heijn, Jumbo), electronics retailers (MediaMarkt, Coolblue), or mobile operator stores throughout Amsterdam city center
All major providers offer excellent 4G coverage in Amsterdam with 5G available in most areas - expect consistent speeds of 20-100 Mbps in urban areas
European roaming is included in most Dutch plans, making it convenient if you plan to visit other EU countries during your trip
Lebara and Lycamobile offer budget-friendly options specifically for international travelers, with SIM cards available at tobacco shops and convenience stores for €5-10
Free WiFi is widely available in Amsterdam at cafes, restaurants, public libraries, and many tourist attractions, reducing dependency on mobile data for basic connectivity
Purchase SIM cards from official stores or authorized retailers to avoid activation issues - carry your passport as ID verification is required for SIM card registration
Consider buying a European multi-country eSIM if visiting multiple countries, with providers like Orange Holiday Europe offering 20GB for €39.99 valid across 30+ European countries

transport_options.description: NS trains connect Amsterdam to other Dutch cities and regions
Amsterdam trams cover the city center and surrounding areas
Regional and local buses connect Amsterdam to nearby areas
Direct train from Schiphol Airport to Haarlem Station

health_tips: No vaccines are required for Indian travelers entering the Netherlands unless arriving from a yellow fever endemic area within 6 days of travel
Routine vaccines should be up-to-date: MMR (measles-mumps-rubella), DPT (diphtheria-pertussis-tetanus), chickenpox, polio, and yearly flu shot
Recommended vaccines include Hepatitis A and B, especially if you plan extended stays or have underlying health conditions
Consider Typhoid vaccination if you plan to visit rural areas or stay with friends/relatives in less developed accommodations
Tap water in Amsterdam is safe to drink and meets high EU standards - no need for bottled water or purification tablets
Food safety standards are excellent in the Netherlands - restaurants and street food are generally safe to consume
Wash hands regularly, especially before eating and after using public transport
Pack a basic first aid kit with pain relievers, anti-diarrheal medication, and any personal prescription medicines
Bring extra prescription medications in original containers, along with a copy of your prescription
European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) is not valid for Indian citizens - ensure you have comprehensive travel health insurance
Emergency medical care is available by calling 112 - hospitals in Amsterdam have English-speaking staff
Pharmacies (apotheek) are widely available for minor health issues and over-the-counter medications
No special precautions needed for mosquito-borne diseases - standard insect repellent sufficient for comfort
Weather can be unpredictable - pack layers and rain gear to avoid getting cold and wet
Air quality in Amsterdam is generally good, but those with respiratory conditions should monitor daily air quality reports
